subject: maths
title: Statistics

nodes.title: Find mean, mode and median of grouped data
nodes.brief: Given a grouped frequency distribution, produce its mean, mode, and median by choosing the right formula for each measure.
nodes.quote: In this chapter, we shall extend the study of these three measures, i.e., mean, median and mode from ungrouped data to that of grouped data.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Choose which measure(s) are required.
Read the question carefully: 'average' or 'typical value' usually means mean; 'middle-most observation' means median; 'most frequent/popular' means mode. Choosing wrongly gives a correct calculation of the wrong thing — always name the measure before computing.

PART 2 — Build the working table.
For the mean, add a class-mark column (xᵢ) and an fᵢxᵢ column (or dᵢ/uᵢ columns for the shortcut methods). For the mode, just identify the modal class. For the median, build a cumulative-frequency column and find n/2.

PART 3 — Apply the correct formula.
Mean: x̄ = Σfᵢxᵢ / Σfᵢ (or the assumed-mean/step-deviation shortcuts). Mode: l + ((f₁−f₀)/(2f₁−f₀−f₂))×h. Median: l + ((n/2 − cf)/f)×h. Substitute carefully.

PART 4 — Interpret the result.
State the value with appropriate units (marks, years, kg, etc.) and, if the question asks for comparison, use the empirical relation 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean to cross-check or estimate a missing measure.
keyMoves: Build the working table (class marks / cf as needed)
misconceptions: Treating grouped-data formulas as identical to ungrouped methods
Using the wrong measure for the situation

nodes.title: Compute the class mark (mid-point) of a class interval
nodes.brief: The class mark xᵢ is the mid-point of a class interval: (lower limit + upper limit) / 2.
nodes.quote: Class mark = (Upper class limit + Lower class limit) / 2
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Add the two class limits.
For a class interval such as 10–25, add the lower limit (10) and the upper limit (25) to get 35. This sum represents twice the mid-point.

PART 2 — Divide by 2 to get the class mark.
Divide the sum by 2: 35 / 2 = 17.5. This value xᵢ = 17.5 represents the entire class in every mean formula. Every row in your mean table needs one class mark computed this way.
keyMoves: Add the lower and upper class limits
misconceptions: Using the lower limit alone as xᵢ

nodes.title: Compute mean of grouped data by the direct method
nodes.brief: Multiply each class mark by its frequency, sum the products, then divide by the total frequency: x̄ = Σfᵢxᵢ / Σfᵢ.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Form the fᵢxᵢ column.
For each class, compute the product fᵢ × xᵢ where xᵢ is the class mark. If a class 10–25 has frequency 3 and class mark 17.5, its product is 52.5. Write this product in a new column alongside each row.

PART 2 — Sum to get Σfᵢxᵢ and Σfᵢ.
Add all the fᵢxᵢ products to get Σfᵢxᵢ. Also add all frequencies to get Σfᵢ (which equals n, the total number of observations). In NCERT Example 1's grouped data, Σfᵢxᵢ = 1860 and Σfᵢ = 30.

PART 3 — Divide Σfᵢxᵢ by Σfᵢ.
The mean x̄ = Σfᵢxᵢ / Σfᵢ = 1860 / 30 = 62. This is the direct method — straightforward but arithmetically heavy for large values.
keyMoves: Sum to get Σfᵢxᵢ and Σfᵢ
misconceptions: Dividing by the number of classes instead of Σfᵢ
Forgetting to multiply xᵢ by fᵢ
Using raw class boundaries instead of class marks

nodes.title: Build the fᵢxᵢ column and its total Σfᵢxᵢ
nodes.brief: Multiply each frequency by its class mark row by row, then total both the fᵢxᵢ column and the fᵢ column.
nodes.quote: Total | Σfᵢ = 30 | | Σfᵢxᵢ = 1860.0
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Row by row, multiply fᵢ × xᵢ.
For each class interval in the table, compute the product of the frequency and the class mark. For example, if f₃ = 6 and x₃ = 32.5, then f₃x₃ = 195. Write this result in the fᵢxᵢ column for that row.

PART 2 — Add the fᵢxᵢ column and add the fᵢ column separately.
Sum all the products in the fᵢxᵢ column to get Σfᵢxᵢ. Independently sum the frequencies to get Σfᵢ = n. Both totals are used in the division x̄ = Σfᵢxᵢ / Σfᵢ. An arithmetic slip in any row corrupts the entire mean, so check each product before summing.
keyMoves: Row by row multiply fᵢ × xᵢ
misconceptions: Omitting a row from the total

nodes.title: Choose an assumed mean 'a'
nodes.brief: Pick a convenient class mark — usually the central one — as the assumed mean a to simplify the arithmetic.
nodes.quote: The first step is to choose one among the xᵢ's as the assumed mean, and denote it by 'a'.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify all the class marks.
List the xᵢ values for every class in the table. These are the only valid candidates for the assumed mean a, since a must be one of the class marks.

PART 2 — Select a central xᵢ as a.
Choose approximately the middle class mark, e.g. a = 47.5 when the marks range from 17.5 to 77.5. A central choice minimises the size of the deviations dᵢ = xᵢ − a, making the arithmetic easier. Importantly, the final mean is the same regardless of which class mark you pick — a is a computational shortcut, not part of the answer.
keyMoves: Select a central xᵢ as a
misconceptions: Believing the answer changes with a different a
Choosing a value that is not a class mark

nodes.title: Compute deviations dᵢ = xᵢ − a
nodes.brief: Subtract the assumed mean a from every class mark to get signed deviations dᵢ.
nodes.quote: i.e., dᵢ = xᵢ – a = xᵢ – 47.5
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Subtract a from each xᵢ.
With a = 47.5, go through every class mark and compute dᵢ = xᵢ − 47.5. Class mark 17.5 gives d = −30; class mark 47.5 gives d = 0; class mark 62.5 gives d = +15. The sign (negative or positive) is essential and must not be dropped.

PART 2 — Keep the sign (negative below a, positive above).
Class marks smaller than a give negative deviations; those above a give positive deviations. These signed values are used in the formula x̄ = a + Σfᵢdᵢ / Σfᵢ. Dropping a negative sign turns a below-average class into an above-average one and corrupts the mean.
keyMoves: Keep the sign (negative below a, positive above)
misconceptions: Computing a − xᵢ instead of xᵢ − a

nodes.title: Compute mean by the assumed mean method
nodes.brief: Use x̄ = a + Σfᵢdᵢ / Σfᵢ where dᵢ = xᵢ − a are the deviations from an assumed mean.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Form fᵢdᵢ and sum it.
For each class, multiply its frequency fᵢ by its deviation dᵢ. The products can be negative. Sum all fᵢdᵢ values to get Σfᵢdᵢ. With a = 47.5 on the NCERT marks data, Σfᵢdᵢ = 435 and Σfᵢ = 30.

PART 2 — Divide Σfᵢdᵢ by Σfᵢ.
Compute the average deviation: 435 / 30 = 14.5.

PART 3 — Add a.
The mean is x̄ = a + (Σfᵢdᵢ / Σfᵢ) = 47.5 + 14.5 = 62. This matches the direct method because x̄ − a equals the average deviation. Never forget to add a back — leaving out a gives only the correction, not the mean.

nodes.title: Compute step deviations uᵢ = (xᵢ − a)/h
nodes.brief: Divide each deviation dᵢ by the class size h to produce small integer step deviations uᵢ.
nodes.quote: So, let uᵢ = (xᵢ – a)/h, where a is the assumed mean and h is the class size.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the class size h.
When all classes have equal width, h is the common width. For classes 10–25, 25–40, 40–55, … with width 15, h = 15. This is the value you will divide by.

PART 2 — Divide each dᵢ by h.
With dᵢ = xᵢ − a already computed, divide by h: uᵢ = dᵢ / h. For dᵢ = −30 and h = 15, uᵢ = −2; for dᵢ = 45, uᵢ = 3. The uᵢ are small integers (or simple fractions for unequal classes), making multiplication by fᵢ much easier. You must later multiply back by h in the final formula.
misconceptions: Forgetting to later multiply by h in the formula

nodes.title: Compute mean by the step-deviation method
nodes.brief: Use x̄ = a + h × (Σfᵢuᵢ / Σfᵢ) where uᵢ = (xᵢ − a)/h.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Form and sum fᵢuᵢ.
Multiply each frequency fᵢ by its step deviation uᵢ. Sum all products to get Σfᵢuᵢ. With a = 47.5, h = 15 on the NCERT marks data, Σfᵢuᵢ = 29 and Σfᵢ = 30.

PART 2 — Divide Σfᵢuᵢ by Σfᵢ.
Compute the average step deviation: 29 / 30.

PART 3 — Multiply by h then add a.
Scale back: x̄ = a + h × (Σfᵢuᵢ / Σfᵢ) = 47.5 + 15 × (29/30) = 47.5 + 14.5 = 62. Forgetting to multiply by h gives a meaninglessly small correction; forgetting to add a gives only the scaled correction.

nodes.title: Apply step-deviation method when class sizes are unequal
nodes.brief: Even with unequal class widths, choose a suitable divisor h and apply x̄ = a + h × (Σfᵢuᵢ / Σfᵢ).
nodes.quote: Here, the class size varies, and the xᵢ are large. Let us still apply the step-deviation method with a = 200 and h = 20.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute class marks of unequal classes.
Find xᵢ = (lower + upper)/2 for each class as usual, even when class widths differ. In NCERT Example 3, the classes have varying widths but each still has a definite mid-point.

PART 2 — Pick a and a suitable divisor h.
Choose a = 200 (a central class mark) and h = 20 (a convenient divisor of most deviations). The uᵢ = (xᵢ − a)/h may be non-integers such as −3.75 when widths differ — that is acceptable.

PART 3 — Apply x̄ = a + h × (Σfᵢuᵢ / Σfᵢ).
With Σfᵢuᵢ = −106 and Σfᵢ = 45: x̄ = 200 + 20 × (−106/45) = 200 − 47.11 = 152.89. Unequal class size does not invalidate the formula; it only means uᵢ are fractions rather than small integers.
keyMoves: Compute class marks of unequal classes
Pick a and a divisor h

nodes.title: Choose an appropriate method to find the mean
nodes.brief: When xᵢ and fᵢ are small use the direct method; when they are large, switch to assumed-mean or step-deviation.
nodes.quote: If xᵢ and fᵢ are sufficiently small, then the direct method is an appropriate choice. If xᵢ and fᵢ are numerically large numbers, then we can go for the assumed mean method or step-deviation method.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Inspect the magnitudes of xᵢ and fᵢ.
Glance at the class marks and frequencies. If both are two-digit numbers or smaller, the direct method (x̄ = Σfᵢxᵢ / Σfᵢ) is manageable. If xᵢ values are in the hundreds or thousands, hand arithmetic becomes error-prone.

PART 2 — Pick the least laborious method.
For large xᵢ: use the assumed-mean method (subtracting a central mark reduces the numbers significantly). If classes are equal-width AND the deviations are multiples of h: use step-deviation (further reduces to small integers uᵢ). All three methods produce the same mean — the choice is purely about minimising arithmetic effort.
keyMoves: Inspect magnitudes of xᵢ and fᵢ
misconceptions: Thinking different methods give different means
Always defaulting to the direct method even for large data

nodes.title: Identify the modal class
nodes.brief: Scan the frequency column for the maximum value; the class interval with that maximum frequency is the modal class.
nodes.quote: Here the maximum class frequency is 8, and the class corresponding to this frequency is 3 – 5. So, the modal class is 3 – 5.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Find the maximum frequency.
Look down the fᵢ column and pick out the largest value. In the NCERT household-size example, the frequencies are 7, 8, 2, 2, 1 — the maximum is 8.

PART 2 — Read off its class interval.
The class corresponding to frequency 8 is 3–5. That class is the modal class. All five parameters for the mode formula (l, h, f₁, f₀, f₂) are extracted relative to this class in the next step.
misconceptions: Picking the class with the largest class mark rather than largest frequency
Confusing modal class with median class

nodes.title: Read off l, h, f₁, f₀, f₂ for the mode formula
nodes.brief: Extract the modal class's lower limit l, class size h, modal frequency f₁, and the frequencies immediately before (f₀) and after (f₂) it.
nodes.quote:
frequency (f₁) of the modal class = 8,
frequency (f₀) of class preceding the modal class = 7,
frequency (f₂) of class succeeding the modal class = 2.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Read l and h from the modal class.
The lower boundary of the modal class is l. If the modal class is 3–5, then l = 3 and h = 5 − 3 = 2. Use actual boundaries (after converting to continuous if needed).

PART 2 — Read f₁, f₀, f₂ from the table.
f₁ is the frequency of the modal class itself (8). f₀ is the frequency of the class immediately before the modal class (7). f₂ is the frequency of the class immediately after (2). 'Before' and 'after' mean the adjacent rows in the table — not the class two rows away.
keyMoves: Read l and h from the modal class
Read f₁, f₀, f₂ from the table
misconceptions: Using the upper limit as l

nodes.title: Apply the grouped-data mode formula
nodes.brief: Substitute l, f₁, f₀, f₂, h into Mode = l + ((f₁−f₀)/(2f₁−f₀−f₂)) × h.
nodes.quote: Mode = l + ( (f₁ – f₀) / (2f₁ – f₀ – f₂) ) × h
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Substitute l, f₁, f₀, f₂, h.
For NCERT Example 5 (household-size data): l = 3, h = 2, f₁ = 8, f₀ = 7, f₂ = 2.

PART 2 — Evaluate the denominator 2f₁ − f₀ − f₂.
The denominator is 2(8) − 7 − 2 = 16 − 9 = 7. Note the formula subtracts both f₀ and f₂ from 2f₁ — a common error is to add f₂ instead.

PART 3 — Compute the fraction, multiply by h, then add l.
Numerator = 8 − 7 = 1. Fraction = 1/7. Multiply by h: (1/7) × 2 = 2/7 ≈ 0.286. Add l: Mode = 3 + 2/7 ≈ 3.286.
keyMoves: Substitute l, f₁, f₀, f₂, h
Compute the fraction and multiply by h
misconceptions: Multiplying l (not the fraction) by h

nodes.title: Build a less-than cumulative frequency column
nodes.brief: Starting from the first class, add each successive frequency to the running total to build the cumulative frequency (cf) column.
nodes.quote: The cumulative frequency of a class is the frequency obtained by adding the frequencies of all the classes preceding the given class.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Start with the first frequency.
The cf of the first class equals its own frequency. If the first class 10–20 has frequency 5, then its cf is 5.

PART 2 — Add each next frequency cumulatively.
For each subsequent class, cf = previous cf + current fᵢ. Example: if frequencies are 5, 3, 4, 3, …, then the cf column is 5, 8, 12, 15, … The final cf must equal n = Σfᵢ, which is a built-in check. This cf column is used both to identify the median class (find n/2 in the cf column) and to read the parameter 'cf' in the median formula.

nodes.title: Recover class frequencies from a cumulative-frequency table
nodes.brief: Given a cumulative-frequency table, subtract consecutive cf values to recover each class's frequency.
nodes.quote: Therefore, the number of girls with height in the interval 140 - 145 = 11 – 4 = 7.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the class boundaries from the upper limits.
A 'less than' cf table lists upper limits (less than 145, less than 150, …). The class 140–145 spans from the previous upper limit to the current one.

PART 2 — Subtract the previous cf from each cf.
Frequency of class = cf of this class − cf of the previous class. From NCERT Example 6: cf below 145 = 11, cf below 140 = 4, so frequency of 140–145 = 11 − 4 = 7. Perform this subtraction for every class. The first class's frequency equals its cf directly.
keyMoves: Identify the class boundaries from the upper limits
Subtract the previous cf from each cf
misconceptions: Treating cumulative values as the frequencies

nodes.title: Identify the median class using n/2
nodes.brief: Compute n/2, then find the class whose cumulative frequency is the smallest value greater than or equal to n/2.
nodes.quote: We now locate the class whose cumulative frequency is greater than (and nearest to) n/2. This is called the median class.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute n/2.
Add all frequencies to get n, then halve it. If n = 53, then n/2 = 26.5. This is the 'target' value you will hunt for in the cf column.

PART 2 — Find the smallest cf that is ≥ n/2, and name that class the median class.
Scan down the cumulative frequency column. The first cf value that equals or exceeds 26.5 identifies the median class. In the NCERT marks example, the cf column is …22, 29, 40… — 29 is the first value ≥ 26.5, so the class 60–70 (with cf 29) is the median class.
keyMoves: Find the smallest cf that is ≥ n/2
Name that class the median class
misconceptions: Picking the class with maximum frequency instead
Choosing the cf that exceeds n/2 by the most rather than the nearest

nodes.title: Read off l, cf, f, h for the median formula
nodes.brief: From the identified median class, extract its lower limit l, class size h, its own frequency f, and the cumulative frequency cf of the class immediately before it.
nodes.quote:
where l = lower limit of median class,
n = number of observations,
cf = cumulative frequency of class preceding the median class,
f = frequency of median class,
h = class size (assuming class size to be equal).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Read l and h from the median class.
The lower boundary of the median class is l. If the median class is 60–70, then l = 60 and h = 10. Use actual boundaries (continuous classes).

PART 2 — Read f of the median class and cf of the preceding class.
f is the frequency of the median class itself (not its cf). cf is the cumulative frequency of the class just before the median class — not the median class's own cf. In the marks example: median class 60–70 has f = 7; the class before it has cf = 22, so cf (parameter) = 22.
keyMoves: Read l and h from the median class
Read f of the median class
Read cf of the preceding class
misconceptions: Using the cf of the median class itself instead of the preceding class

nodes.title: Apply the grouped-data median formula
nodes.brief: Substitute l, n, cf, f, h into Median = l + ((n/2 − cf)/f) × h.
nodes.quote: Median = l + ( ((n/2) – cf) / f ) × h,
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Substitute n/2, cf, f, h, l.
For the NCERT marks example: l = 60, n/2 = 26.5, cf = 22 (cf of the class before the median class), f = 7, h = 10.

PART 2 — Compute n/2 − cf, then divide by f and multiply by h.
26.5 − 22 = 4.5. Then (4.5 / 7) × 10 = 45/7 ≈ 6.43.

PART 3 — Add l.
Median = 60 + 6.43 = 66.43 ≈ 66.4. Never use n (total) instead of n/2, and never add cf instead of subtracting it — either error gives a wildly wrong answer.
keyMoves: Substitute n/2, cf, f, h, l
Divide by f and multiply by h

nodes.title: Convert inclusive (discontinuous) classes to continuous classes
nodes.brief: When class intervals like 118–126, 127–135 have a gap, shift each boundary by half the gap (0.5) to make them continuous before applying mode or median formulas.
nodes.quote: For calculating mode and median for grouped data, it should be ensured that the class intervals are continuous before applying the formulae.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Find the gap between consecutive class limits.
Examine two adjacent classes, e.g. 118–126 and 127–135. The upper limit of the first is 126 and the lower limit of the second is 127 — gap = 1. Half the gap = 0.5.

PART 2 — Subtract half the gap from lower limits and add to upper limits.
Adjust every boundary: 118 − 0.5 = 117.5 becomes the new lower limit; 126 + 0.5 = 126.5 becomes the new upper limit; and so on. The adjusted classes 117.5–126.5, 126.5–135.5, … are continuous. Now l in the mode/median formula is the adjusted lower boundary, not the original one.
keyMoves: Find the gap between consecutive class limits
Subtract half the gap from lower limits and add to upper limits
misconceptions: Applying formulas directly to gapped classes
Shifting by the full gap instead of half

nodes.title: Find missing frequencies given the median and total
nodes.brief: When two frequencies x and y are unknown, use Σf = n (one equation) and the median formula at the given median value (second equation) to solve for both.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Form cumulative frequencies in terms of x.
Express the cf column symbolically. If x is the missing frequency of a particular class, every cf at and below it involves x. This lets you write 'cf of the class before the median class' as an expression in x.

PART 2 — Write the x + y = k equation from Σf = n.
Sum all frequencies (some with x and y) and set equal to n. In NCERT Example 8, x + y + (others) = 100 gives x + y = 24.

PART 3 — Substitute into the median formula at the given median to solve for x, then y.
Median = 525 is given; the median class is 500–600. Substituting: 525 = 500 + ((50 − 36 − x)/20) × 100 gives x = 9. Back-substitute: y = 24 − 9 = 15.
keyMoves: Form cumulative frequencies in terms of x
Substitute into median formula at given median
misconceptions: Using the wrong cf in the median formula

nodes.title: Use the empirical relation 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean
nodes.brief: The empirical relationship 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean lets you estimate one measure when the other two are known.
nodes.quote: 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Substitute the two known measures.
If Mean = 35.4 and Median = 35.75, substitute into 3 × Median = Mode + 2 × Mean: 3 × 35.75 = Mode + 2 × 35.4 → 107.25 = Mode + 70.8 → Mode = 36.45.

PART 2 — Solve for the unknown one.
Rearrange as needed: Mode = 3 Median − 2 Mean; or Median = (Mode + 2 Mean)/3; or Mean = (3 Median − Mode)/2. Remember: this is an empirical approximation, not an exact algebraic law, so it is used for estimation or cross-checking, not as a proof.
misconceptions: Mixing up which measure has the coefficient 3 vs 2
Treating the relation as exact for all data

nodes.title: Select the appropriate measure of central tendency
nodes.brief: Choose mean when all values contribute equally, median when extremes distort the picture, or mode when the most frequent value matters.
nodes.quote: In situations which require establishing the most frequent value or most popular item, the mode is the best choice
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify whether extreme values distort the mean.
The mean uses every observation, so one very high or very low value shifts it significantly. In such cases — exam scores with a few outliers, income data — the median better represents a typical observation because it is unaffected by extremes.

PART 2 — Decide if a typical or a most-frequent value is needed.
If the question asks for the most popular shoe size, the most common commute time, or the most sold item — mode is required. If it asks for the 'average' in a balanced dataset, the mean works well. If it asks for the middle-most observation, choose median.

PART 3 — Pick mean, median, or mode accordingly.
State your choice and justify it briefly. 'We use mode because we want the most frequently occurring household size' is a complete, exam-worthy answer.
keyMoves: Identify whether extremes distort the mean
Decide if a typical or a most-frequent value is wanted
Pick mean, median, or mode accordingly
misconceptions: Thinking the median is affected by extreme values like the mean

narrative:
## What I audited

I re-read the full segmented text of jemh113 ("Statistics"), SEG 0 through SEG 4, every worked Example (1-8), the three mean methods, the mode/median formulas and the summary, plus the shared CBSE Maths SQP + Marking Scheme (`_maths-exams.txt`) for the four exam-sourced items. I then opened the exact segment for EVERY node and EVERY gate and located the quote verbatim.

## Corrections made to the draft

**Citation (sec) fixes — the #1 failure mode.** Two node quotes were tagged to the wrong segment:
- `compute-cumulative-frequency-less-than` — quote "The cumulative frequency of a class is the frequency obtained by adding the frequencies of all the classes preceding the given class." is NOT in SEG 3; it is verbatim in the SEG 4 Summary (point 3). Re-tagged sec 3 → 4. (Its gate "Less than 30 | 8 + 4 = 12" is genuinely in SEG 3 and was left.)
- `convert-discontinuous-to-continuous-classes` — quote "For calculating mode and median for grouped data, it should be ensured that the class intervals are continuous before applying the formulae." is in SEG 4's "A NOTE TO THE READER", not SEG 3. Re-tagged sec 3 → 4. (Its gate quote about 117.5-126.5 is in the SEG 3 Exercise hint and was left.)

**Dropped a node with no answer-keyed gate.** `find-unknown-frequency-from-known-mean` mapped to Exercise 13.1 Q3 (mean = Rs 18, find missing frequency f). The chapter never works this out — there is no stated answer, and no exam item covers it. Its draft gate had been borrowed from Example 8's "i.e., y = 15", which is actually the SECOND output of the median-based two-unknowns skill (`find-unknown-frequencies-from-median`), not a mean-based result. I removed the node, its two edges (to the goal and to `compute-mean-direct-method`), and that borrowed y=15 gate, keeping a single clean inverse-frequency node (`find-unknown-frequencies-from-median`, Example 8, gated on x=9). Moved the absent mean-based variant to absentNotes.

**Minor gate-prompt fix.** The `identify-modal-class` gate called Example 5's data "female-size data"; it is household family-size data. Corrected the prompt wording (quote unchanged, still verbatim).

## Verified verbatim (spot list)
- goal (sec 0) "...extend the study of these three measures, i.e., mean, median and mode from ungrouped data to that of grouped data." ✓ line 7
- `apply-mode-formula` (sec 2) "Mode = l + ( (f₁ – f₀) / (2f₁ – f₀ – f₂) ) × h" ✓ line 323; gate = CBSE Basic SQP Q25 "= 18.21(approx.)" ✓
- `apply-median-formula` (sec 3) "Median = l + ( ((n/2) – cf) / f ) × h," ✓ line 548; gate "Median = 145 + ( (25.5 – 11) / 18 ) × 5..." ✓
- `apply-step-deviation-unequal-classes` gate "Therefore, x̄ = 200 + 20 ((–106)/45) = 200 – 47.11 = 152.89." ✓ line 249
- exam gates: Q9 "9. C) 30 1" ✓, Q16 "16. A) median 1" ✓ (question "Which of the following gives the middle most observation of the data? A) Median..." ✓ line 115)

## Edges
Single root `find-measures-of-central-tendency-grouped-data`. After dropping the orphaned mean-inverse node, all 24 nodes are in at least one edge, every endpoint is a defined slug, the graph is acyclic, and the three mean methods / mode / median / method-selection / empirical relation / median-inverse all hang off the root with their tabulation sub-skills decomposing down to bedrock.

## Gates
24 gates, one per node (100% coverage, including the goal node's mode=52 gate). All graders are numeric (with tol) or mcq; each answer is self-consistent with its expected block; every quote was confirmed a contiguous character-for-character substring of its tagged segment.

## What was absent / uncertain
- Ogives are named in SEG 0 and SEG 4 but never demonstrated (no plotting/construction/worked example) — no node, recorded in absentNotes.
- Finding a missing frequency from a GIVEN MEAN (Exercise 13.1 Q3) is a real skill but unworked in this chapter and absent from the supplied exams, so it has no answer-keyed gate — dropped and noted.
absentNotes:
OGIVES: the introduction (SEG 0) and the closing "NOTE TO THE READER" (SEG 4) reference ogives (less-than and more-than cumulative frequency curves) and finding the median graphically from their intersection, but THIS transcription contains no worked example, no plotting steps and no construction procedure, so no ogive node was created. A fuller treatment would teach plotting a less-than ogive, plotting a more-than ogive, and locating the median at their intersection (or via the n/2 horizontal line).

FIND MISSING FREQUENCY FROM A GIVEN MEAN: Exercise 13.1 Q3 ("The mean pocket allowance is Rs 18. Find the missing frequency f") poses this genuinely-distinct inverse skill, but the chapter never solves it (it is an exercise with no stated answer) and no supplied CBSE exam item covers it, so there is no answer-keyed gate available. The draft's node for this had borrowed Example 8's median-based "y = 15" result; that was incorrect provenance, so the node was dropped. The chapter DOES fully work the median-based two-unknowns version (Example 8), retained as `find-unknown-frequencies-from-median`.

MORE-THAN-TYPE cumulative frequency (Table 13.14) is constructed but never used to compute any measure, so only the less-than construction is a node.

DELIBERATELY DEFERRED BY THE TEXT (correctly omitted): mode/median for grouped data with UNEQUAL class sizes ("we shall not be discussing it"), and MULTIMODAL data ("we shall restrict ourselves to problems having a single mode only").

Derivations of d̄ = x̄ − a and hū = x̄ − a are exposition justifying the mean formulas, not separately failable student skills, so they are folded into the corresponding method nodes rather than split out.
